title.value: OTC: Optimal Tool Calls via Reinforcement Learning
title.evidence: OTC: Optimal Tool Calls via Reinforcement Learning

authors.evidence: Hongru Wangα, Cheng Qianβ, Wanjun Zhongδ, Xiusi Chenβ, Jiahao Qiuσ, Shijue Huangµ, Bowen Jinβ, Mengdi Wangσ, Kam-Fai Wongα, Heng Jiβ

value: The Chinese University of Hong Kong
Hong Kong University of Science and Technology
affiliations.evidence: αThe Chinese University of Hong Kong, βUniversity of Illinois Urbana-Champaign σPrinceton University, $^ \delta \mathsf { S u n }$ Yat-sen University, $\mu$ Hong Kong University of Science and Technology

current_issues.value: Existing approaches often overlook the efficiency and cost associated with tool usage, leading to excessive tool calls that increase computational and financial overhead.
current_issues.evidence: While reinforcement learning (RL) has shown promise in improving TIR by optimizing final answer correctness, existing approaches often overlook the efficiency and cost associated with tool usage.

target.value: Develop a method that optimizes tool usage efficiency while maintaining answer correctness.
target.evidence: We aim to develop such a method based on a foundational assumption: for each question and each model, there exists an optimal number of tool calls, defined as the minimal number required for the model to arrive at a correct answer.

framework_innovation.value: Optimal Tool Call-controlled Policy Optimization (OTC-PO) framework that encourages accurate answers with minimal tool calls.
framework_innovation.evidence: We propose Optimal Tool Call-controlled Policy Optimization (OTC-PO), a simple yet effective RL-based framework that encourages models to produce accurate answers with minimal tool calls.

metric_innovation.value: Introduction of tool productivity as a measure of effectiveness and efficiency in tool-integrated reasoning.
metric_innovation.evidence: We introduce the concept of tool productivity to measure the effectiveness and efficiency of TIR.

algorithm_innovation.value: Integration of OTC-PO into existing RL algorithms like PPO and GRPO.
algorithm_innovation.evidence: We implement OTC-PPO and OTC-GRPO as two typical methods without losing the adaptability and generalization.

performance_improvement.value: Reduces tool calls by up to 73.1% and improves tool productivity by up to 229.4% while maintaining comparable answer accuracy.
performance_improvement.evidence: Experiments with Qwen-2.5 and Qwen-Math across multiple QA benchmarks show that our approach reduces tool calls by up to 73.1% and improves tool productivity by up to 229.4% while maintaining comparable answer accuracy.

tool_call_reduction.value: OTC-PO reduces tool calls significantly while maintaining accuracy.
tool_call_reduction.evidence: OTC-PPO achieves significant improvement in terms of TC and TP compared with Search-R1.

model_size_effect.value: Larger models tend to over-rely on external tool calls unless penalized for such behavior.
model_size_effect.evidence: As the model size increases, the TC and TP get bigger boost no matter in OTC-PPO or OTC-GRPO.

generalization_performance.value: OTC-PO maintains generalization capabilities across different benchmarks.
generalization_performance.evidence: OTC-PPO outperforms the Search-R1-PPO in terms of EM on all benchmarks with Qwen2.5-7B-Base model with much less tool calls.

case_study_insights.value: OTC-PO reduces unnecessary tool calls significantly compared to Search-R1.
case_study_insights.evidence: Our method achieves same answers with baseline using fewer tool calls in mostly 80% of test cases.

task_definition.value: Define the task as generating correct answers with minimal tool calls.
task_definition.evidence: The objective of the task is to generate the correct answer with the minimal cost of tools in the full trajectory.

base_reward.evidence: Most prior predominantly focuses on rule-based correctness rewards.

efficiency_reward.value: Tool-integrated reward that modulates correctness with tool usage efficiency.
efficiency_reward.evidence: We regard the tool-integrated reward as a coefficient of conventional reward function.

provenance.section_header: 3.3 OTC-PO: Optimal Tool Call-controlled Policy Optimization

final_reward.value: Combined reward that incorporates both correctness and tool efficiency.
final_reward.evidence: The final tool-integrated reward function is a multiplicative structure that ensures tool efficiency is mainly rewarded when the primary task objective is satisfied.

provenance.section_header: 3.3 OTC-PO: Optimal Tool Call-controlled Policy Optimization

algorithm_integration.value: Integration of OTC-PO with PPO and GRPO methods.
algorithm_integration.evidence: We instantiate OTC-PO in two variants: OTC-PPO and OTC-GRPO.

provenance.section_header: 3.3 OTC-PO: Optimal Tool Call-controlled Policy Optimization

datasets.value: NQ and HotpotQA for search, and ToRL dataset for code.
datasets.evidence: We mainly follow the Search-R1 and ToRL setting and use same baselines and datasets for the fair and comprehensive evaluation.

models.evidence: Using multiple LLMs—including Qwen-2.5-3B/7B-Base and Qwen2.5-Math-1.5B/7B-Base.

parameters.value: Maximal number of tool calls set to 4 for Search-R1 and 3 for ToRL.
parameters.evidence: We set the max turn (maximal number of tool calls) in Search-R1 (ToRL) to 4 (3).

resources.evidence: We conduct our experiments on 8 A100-80G GPU.

search_tool_results.value: OTC-PPO and OTC-GRPO outperform Search-R1 in terms of tool calls and productivity.
search_tool_results.evidence: OTC-PPO achieves significant improvement in terms of TC and TP compared with Search-R1.

code_tool_results.value: Preliminary results indicate OTC-PO's effectiveness in code tasks.
code_tool_results.evidence: We already observe similar findings as mentioned above and will release more results soon.
code_tool_results.notes: Results for code tasks are preliminary.

behavior_analysis.value: OTC-PO significantly reduces unnecessary tool calls while maintaining accuracy.
behavior_analysis.evidence: Our method achieves same answers with baseline using fewer tool calls in mostly 80% of test cases.
